one_liner: A 78-year-old male with a history of hypertension presents with exertional dyspnea, angina, and a harsh systolic murmur radiating to the carotids.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports a 6-month history of progressive exertional dyspnea, now limiting his ability to walk more than half a block. He has also developed exertional chest tightness and had a near-syncopal episode last week while climbing stairs. He felt lightheaded and had to sit down but did not lose consciousness. His wife notes he has been less active and more easily fatigued. He denies orthopnea, PND, or leg swelling.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension (25 years), hyperlipidemia, bicuspid aortic valve (diagnosed incidentally 15 years ago), osteoporosis
stage2.medications: Amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, calcium/vitamin D supplementation
stage2.family_history: Father died of 'heart disease' at age 72. Mother had aortic valve replacement at age 80.
stage2.social_history: Former pipe smoker (quit 20 years ago). No alcohol. Retired engineer. Lives with wife. Previously active, now limited.

vitals.bp: 128/82 mmHg with narrow pulse pressure (46 mmHg)

stage2.physical_exam: General: Elderly male, appears well at rest. HEENT: Carotid upstrokes are diminished and delayed (pulsus parvus et tardus). Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; harsh, late-peaking crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur (Grade IV/VI) at the right upper sternal border radiating to both carotids; single S2 (absent A2); S4 present; sustained, non-displaced PMI. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Extremities: No edema.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated, indicates myocardial stress from pressure overload
Not elevated, no acute myocardial necrosis
Mildly elevated but within normal for age
Mild anemia — consider acquired von Willebrand syndrome (Heyde syndrome)

imaging_results.result: Severely calcified bicuspid aortic valve. Peak velocity 4.8 m/s, mean gradient 52 mmHg, aortic valve area 0.7 cm². Concentric LVH. LVEF 55%. No significant mitral regurgitation. Ascending aorta mildly dilated (4.0 cm).
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Severe aortic stenosis by all criteria (velocity >4 m/s, mean gradient >40 mmHg, AVA <1.0 cm²)

imaging_results.result: Normal sinus rhythm, LVH with strain pattern (ST depression and T-wave inversion in I, aVL, V5-V6), left atrial enlargement
imaging_results.clinical_significance: LVH with strain from chronic pressure overload

imaging_results.result: Boot-shaped heart (concentric LVH), calcification of aortic valve visible, no pulmonary edema
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Calcific aortic valve and LVH without decompensation

acceptable_tests: CT calcium scoring of aortic valve
stage3.key_tests_rationale: Echo is the gold standard for diagnosing and grading AS severity (velocity, gradient, valve area). ECG shows LVH. BNP correlates with prognosis. BMP checks renal function pre-procedure. CBC screens for anemia (Heyde syndrome).

stage5.final_clue: Echocardiography reveals a severely calcified bicuspid aortic valve with a peak velocity of 4.8 m/s, mean gradient of 52 mmHg, and valve area of 0.7 cm² — meeting all criteria for severe aortic stenosis. The classic triad of angina, syncope, and dyspnea confirms symptomatic severe AS.

debrief.classic_presentation: Severe aortic stenosis presents with the classic triad of exertional angina, syncope, and heart failure (dyspnea). Physical exam reveals a harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids, pulsus parvus et tardus, narrow pulse pressure, and an S4. Late peaking of the murmur indicates more severe stenosis.
debrief.pathophysiology: Progressive calcification and fibrosis of the aortic valve (accelerated in bicuspid valves) reduce the valve orifice, creating a fixed obstruction to LV outflow. The LV compensates with concentric hypertrophy to maintain wall stress and cardiac output. Eventually, the hypertrophied myocardium outgrows its blood supply (angina), becomes non-compliant (diastolic dysfunction, HF), and cannot augment output with exercise (syncope).
debrief.treatment_overview: Symptomatic severe AS requires valve replacement — medical therapy alone carries high mortality (50% at 2 years for HF symptoms). Options: surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) for low-surgical-risk patients, or transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) for intermediate-to-high-risk patients. No medical therapy slows progression. Avoid vasodilators (can cause severe hypotension). Coronary angiography is performed pre-operatively.
high_yield_pearls: The classic symptom triad of severe AS is angina, syncope, and heart failure — once symptoms develop, prognosis worsens rapidly without intervention.
Pulsus parvus et tardus (weak, delayed carotid upstroke) is the most reliable physical exam finding correlating with severe AS.
Severe AS criteria on echo: peak velocity >4 m/s, mean gradient >40 mmHg, valve area <1.0 cm².
Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common cause of AS in patients <70 years; degenerative calcification in patients >70 years.
Heyde syndrome is the association of severe AS with GI bleeding from acquired von Willebrand syndrome (high shear stress destroys large vWF multimers) and angiodysplasia.
sources: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
Otto et al., Aortic Stenosis, NEJM 2020

after_labs_top3: Bicuspid aortic valve with severe AS
